properties.name: C-Deck Hibernation Sector
properties.description: The hibernation and recovery wing of C-Deck. Rows of bays holding sleeping colonists, connected by service corridors to medical and support facilities.
properties.aiPrompt: This is the starting area — the player's first impression of the ship. It should feel functional and institutional: the original ship designers built this for a purpose and it still mostly serves that purpose. But even here, there are hints of the centuries that passed — murals painted over older murals, repurposed equipment, small personal touches left by the people who maintained these bays generation after generation. The tone here is restrained. The weirdness and diversity of the ship should be hinted at, not fully revealed. Let the player feel safe enough to explore, curious enough to keep going. Think of this as the tutorial area — not in a gamey way, but in the sense that the player is learning how the world works before encountering its full strangeness.

properties.name: Botanical Quarter
properties.description: A sprawling section of the ship given over to plant cultivation, long since overgrown into something between a greenhouse and a jungle.
properties.aiPrompt: This is where the ship gets weird for the first time. A community of plant breeders lived here for centuries, and they went far — absurdly, beautifully, sometimes disturbingly far. They bred plants that glow, plants that move, plants that seem to respond to sound, plants that grow in geometric patterns that shouldn't be possible. The infrastructure is still visible — grow-lights, irrigation pipes, nutrient tanks — but it's been swallowed by the results of a thousand years of obsessive horticulture. There should be humor here: plants bred for purely aesthetic or philosophical reasons, labels on grow-troughs with pretentious cultivar names, a community that clearly took itself very seriously while doing something inherently ridiculous. But there should also be genuine wonder — some of what they created is breathtaking. The deeper you go, the stranger it gets. Robots here might be garden tenders that have been maintaining and possibly continuing the breeding programs on their own.
